repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body: Allows configuring them independently rather than in one big matrix.

comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -139,146 +148,206 @@ jobs:
             python-version: 3.14t
             backend: "cpp"
 
+    uses: ./.github/workflows/ci-job.yml
+    with:
+      os: ${{ matrix.os }}
+      python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
+      backend: ${{ matrix.backend }}
+
+
+  ci-arm64:
+    # ARM builds
+    needs: [ci-early-success]
+
+    strategy:
+      # Allows for matrix sub-jobs to fail without canceling the rest
+      fail-fast: false
+      matrix:
+        os: [ubuntu-24.04-arm, windows-11-arm]
+        backend: [c, cpp]
+        python-version:
+          - "3.13"
+
+    uses: ./.github/workflows/ci-job.yml
+    with:
+      os: ${{ matrix.os }}
+      python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
+      backend: ${{ matrix.backend }}
+
+
+  ci-typespecs:
+    # compile with typespecs
+    needs: [ci-early-success]
+
+    strategy:
+      # Allows for matrix sub-jobs to fail without canceling the rest
+      fail-fast: false
+      matrix:
+        python-version:
+          - "3.9"
+          - "3.12"
+          - "3.14"
+        backend: [c]
+        os: [ubuntu-24.04]
+
+    uses: ./.github/workflows/ci-job.yml
+    with:
+      os: ${{ matrix.os }}
+      python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
+      backend: ${{ matrix.backend }}
+      extra_cflags: "-DCYTHON_USE_TYPE_SPECS=1"
+      extra_hash: "-typespecs"
+
+
+  ci-sharedmodule:
+    # compile with shared utility module
+    needs: [ci-early-success]
+
+    strategy:
+      # Allows for matrix sub-jobs to fail without canceling the rest
+      fail-fast: false
+      matrix:
+        backend: [c,cpp,"c,cpp"]

comment_data.body: I was thinking that it would be good to test that the module can be shared between both variants of modules. But looking it up now, the shared module is _always_ generated as C module, so the C++ test run already validates that. I'll remove the case.

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
        backend: [c]
```

I know this is just copying what we already have. However, I wonder if it's worth dropping `cpp` here.

In that `compile-all` failure are almost always at the "build cython" stage (which is always done in C anyway).

I don't think I've ever seen a failure here were something breaks only on the cpp tests and only on the "compile-all" Cython.

+    with:
+      os: ${{ matrix.os }}
+      python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}

+      extra_cflags: "-DCYTHON_USE_TYPE_SPECS=1"
+      extra_hash: "-typespecs"
+

+  ci-sharedmodule:
+    # compile with shared utility module
+    needs: [ci-early-success]
+
+    strategy:
+      # Allows for matrix sub-jobs to fail without canceling the rest
+      fail-fast: false
+      matrix:
+        backend: [c,cpp]
+        python-version:
+          - "3.12"
+          #- "3.14"  # -> 'ci-early'
+        os: [ubuntu-24.04]
+
+    uses: ./.github/workflows/ci-job.yml
+    with:
+      os: ${{ matrix.os }}
+      python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
+      backend: ${{ matrix.backend }}
+      shared_utility: "--shared-utility"
+      extra_hash: "-shared-utility"
+
+
+  ci-compileall:
+    # compile all modules
+    needs: [ci-early-success]
+
+    strategy:
+      # Allows for matrix sub-jobs to fail without canceling the rest
+      fail-fast: false
+      matrix:
+        backend: [c, cpp]

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
          - "3.x"
```

Slight change to what we already have, but I wonder if "latest release version" is a good choice for the arm tests
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -139,146 +148,213 @@ jobs:
             python-version: 3.14t
             backend: "cpp"
 
+    uses: ./.github/workflows/ci-job.yml
+    with:
+      os: ${{ matrix.os }}

+      backend: ${{ matrix.backend }}

+        backend: [c, cpp]
+        python-version:
+          - "3.13"

comment_data.body: Still, it's the only time where we test Cython with all (reasonably) compiled modules. Excluding the C++ tests would mean that we don't test that part of the code in its compiled form. I haven't looked closely, but I doubt that there is much C++ related code that gets compiled, so your question is certainly worth considering. But I'd lean towards keeping it covered regardless.

commit.message: CI: Split special jobs into separate groups to configure them independently rather than in one big matrix.

title: CI: Split special jobs into separate groups.
